coredata.dc:title: Sugar-originated carbon nanodots selectively damage the tumor and enhance the sensitivity of chemotherapy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi
Huo, Taotao
Jiang, Huiling
Xie, Yibo
Zhang, Xiaoyi
Nie, Huifang
Yang, Yafeng
Qian, Min
Li, Wenshuai
Hao, Tingting
Guo, Wei
Qin, Yanhui
Shi, Jinlong
Shi, Wei
Huang, Rongqin
coredata.dc:description: Blocking the tumor energy metabolism is expected to be a potential cancer treatment strategy. However, developing a single and simple nanoparticle system to directly block the tumor energy metabolism with good biosafety and high potential for clinical application remains absence. Herein, a technique is described for the preparation of sugar-originated carbon nanodots (sCND) which is inspired by the traditional cooking skill of pork braised in brown sauce, where the sucrose caramelizes in the hot vegetable oil. The special preparation endows sCND with some glucose residues on their surfaces and optical nanodot properties, which enable the GLUT1-mediated glioma-targeted accumulation that can be monitored by in vivo imaging. Subsequently, sCND can hamper the avid uptake of glucose into the tumor. This blocks the tumor energy metabolism, impairs tumor growth and enhances sensitivity to chemotherapy as shown in two tumor models (glioma and hepatoma). Additionally, this kind of carbon nanodots possesses a favorable biosafety profile, including low cytotoxicity, almost no hematotoxicity and tissue damaging potential. It is edible due to the special preparation and structure similar to the traditional cooking style. This work reports a simple method to produce advanced therapeutic carbon nanodots with favorable biodistribution and biosafety, which will pave the way for a powerful platform with future clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sugar-originated carbon nanodots
GLUT1
Chemosensitization

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence intensities of U87 cells or 1800 cells incubated with 200 μg/mL sCND for 2 h after different treatments (n = 3), GLU-free: glucose-free medium, GLU: D-glucose, Com-M: complete media, CYB: Cytochalasin B. All groups used the glucose-free medium, and only Com-M contains D-glucose (4.5 mg/mL). (b) Confocal fluorescence images of U87 cells incubated with sCND and TRITC-labeled GLUT1 antibody for different time periods. BF: bright-field images. Blue and red indicate the presence of sCND and GLUT1, respectively. (c) Real-time monitoring of the frequency changes and dissipation factors on different cells-modified QCM sensors during sCND adhesion. (d) Confocal fluorescence images of U87 cells stained by glucose fluorescent probes (6-NBDG, green) after incubation with different concentrations of sCND for 2 h. Scale bar = 200 µm. (e) Amounts of lactic acid in normal and cancer cells after incubation with different concentrations of sCND for 4 h (n = 3). (f) Positron emission tomography (PET) and computed tomography (CT) scans of the orthotopic glioma-bearing mice at 15 and 30 min post-intravenous injection with different concentrations of sCND. Before treated by sCND, the mice were subjected a 15 min pre-intravenous injection of [18F]FDG for glucose imaging. Data are represented as mean ± SD. Scale bar = 25 µm.

(a) The binding structure modes and (b) schematic diagrams of glucose-GLUT1 with different magnifications based on molecules docking studies. (c) The binding structure modes and (d) Schematic diagrams of sCND-GLUT1 with different magnifications based on molecules docking studies. (e) CDocker energy between GLUT1 and glucose or sCND via the molecular docking studies (n = 10). (f) Schematic illustration of the sCND-interfered glucose transportation and metabolization. (g) Circular dichroism spectra of GLUT-1 before and after interaction with sCND or glucose. (h) AFM images with the corresponding roughness index (Rq) and (i) the fluidity via FRAP analysis of the normal and cancer cell membranes after incubation with or without sCND. Diff Co and t1/2 represent the diffusion coefficient and half-time for the fluorescence recovery of membrane after photobleaching, respectively. Data are represented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

(a) Real-time cell analyzer (RTCA)-generated time-dependent cell growth response profiles of U87 and 1800 cells incubated with or without sCND after the starvation for 12 h. (b) cell viabilities of tumor (HepG2) and normal (HL 7702) cells incubated with different concentrations of sCND for varied time periods under the glucose-contained (+) or glucose-free (-) culture media. (c) Intracellular ATP content of tumor (HepG2) and normal (HL 7702) cells after different sCND treatments under the glucose-contained (+) or glucose-free (-) culture media. Pre-starvation: the adherent cells were incubated with glucose-free media for 4 h before sCND treatments (n = 4). (d) Confocal fluorescence images of tumor (HepG2) and normal (HL 7702) cells incubated with different concentrations of sCND for varied time periods under the glucose-contained (+) or glucose-free (-) culture media. (Green: Live; Red: Dead; n = 4). Scale bar = 100 µm. (e) Cytotoxicity and (f) Confocal images of U87 cells treated by sCND alone or TMZ combined with different concentrations of sCND. Green: live cells; Red: dead cells (n = 4). Scale bar = 100 µm. (g) Average body weight and (h) Kaplan–Meier survival curves of the orthotopic glioma-bearing mice after different treatments (n = 8). (i) Glioma apoptosis results via TUNEL detection kit on 15th day post injection of sCND alone or TMZ combined with different amounts of sCND. Blue: DAPI-labeled cell nuclei; green: FITC-labeled apoptotic cells; Yellow dotted lines indicate borders of glioma. (j) Cytotoxicity for various types of cells under different treatments (n = 4). (k) Cytotoxicity of MCF-7 and MCF-10 under different treatments (n = 4). The inset in (k) is the therapeutic schedule of sCND as a chemotherapeutic sensitizer. Data are represented as mean ± SD.

(a) Therapeutic schedules of sCND (i.v., 2 mg)-enhanced sorafenib (oral, 25 mg/kg) chemotherapies for Hepatoma, Sorafenib were given by gavage and sCND were given by the intravenous route. (b) Average body weight, (c) Optical images of tumor, (d) The tumor growth curves, and (e) Tumor weight of the subcutaneous hepatoma-bearing mice after different treatments as illustrated in “a” (n = 8). (f) The apoptosis results via TUNEL detection of the subcutaneous hepatoma-bearing mice after different treatments as illustrated in “a” and “g”. Blue: DAPI-labeled cell nuclei; green: FITC-labeled apoptotic cells. Scale bar = 100 µm. (g) Therapeutic schedule of the sCND (oral, 2 mg) -enhanced sorafenib (oral, 25 mg/kg) for Hepatoma, both sorafenib and sCND were given by gavage, In group A, the tumor-bearing mice were given normal saline by mouth. (h) Average body weight, (i) Optical images of tumor, (j) The tumor growth curves and (k) Tumor weight of the subcutaneous hepatoma-bearing mice after different treatments as illustrated in “g” (n = 8). Data are represented as mean ± SD.

Blood biochemical indexes of SD rats at different time points after intravenous injection of physiological saline or 200 mg/kg of sCND, respectively. Blood routine: Red blood cells (RBC), White blood cells (WBC), Platelet (PLT), Haemoglobin (HGB), Erythrocyte mean corpuscular volume (MCV), Hematocrit (HCT), Hemoglobin Distribution Width (HDW), Red Cell Distribution Width (RDW), Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC), Erythrocyte mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), Serum biochemical index: the liver function: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), Aspartate transaminase (AST), alanine transaminase (ALT); the kidney function: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CREA); Serum lipid: Cholesterol (CH). Data are represented as mean ± SD (n = 6).
Schematic diagram of the sugar-originated carbon nanodots (sCND) for GLUT1-mediated tumor-targeted imaging and enhanced chemotherapy, TAC Cycle: tricarboxylic acid cycle.

head.ce:title: Sugar-originated carbon nanodots selectively damage the tumor and enhance the sensitivity of chemotherapy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blocking the tumor energy metabolism is expected to be a potential cancer treatment strategy. However, developing a single and simple nanoparticle system to directly block the tumor energy metabolism with good biosafety and high potential for clinical application remains absence. Herein, a technique is described for the preparation of sugar-originated carbon nanodots (sCND) which is inspired by the traditional cooking skill of pork braised in brown sauce, where the sucrose caramelizes in the hot vegetable oil. The special preparation endows sCND with some glucose residues on their surfaces and optical nanodot properties, which enable the GLUT1-mediated glioma-targeted accumulation that can be monitored by in vivo imaging. Subsequently, sCND can hamper the avid uptake of glucose into the tumor. This blocks the tumor energy metabolism, impairs tumor growth and enhances sensitivity to chemotherapy as shown in two tumor models (glioma and hepatoma). Additionally, this kind of carbon nanodots possesses a favorable biosafety profile, including low cytotoxicity, almost no hematotoxicity and tissue damaging potential. It is edible due to the special preparation and structure similar to the traditional cooking style. This work reports a simple method to produce advanced therapeutic carbon nanodots with favorable biodistribution and biosafety, which will pave the way for a powerful platform with future clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle-mediated tumor treatments have received tremendous attentions due to their potentials of augmenting the delivery of therapeutics [1–3]. However, their clinical applications are still in its infancy because of the unrevealed biological effects to tumor cells and the nonnegligible biosafety issues to normal cells [4,5]. It is well known that tumors undergo abnormal glycolysis to survive with a high uptake of glucose mediated by the overexpressed glucose transporters (GLUT) on their surfaces [6,7]. Therefore, a kind of nanoparticles that specifically interact with GLUT and interfere with the glucose uptake will attenuate the energy supply for tumors and thus impair tumor growth ( Scheme 1). Because of the distinct interaction between GLUT and glucose, sugar molecules-derived nanoparticles are speculated to possess the similar interaction with GLUT owing to possible glucose residues at the surface but to be incapable of supplying energy. In this case, sugar molecules-derived nanoparticles would be potential therapeutics alone or in combination with other therapies via inhibition of the tumor-avid glucose uptake. So far, this envisioned therapeutic concept has never been actually exploited, since suitable and biosafety nanoparticles are not available and the corresponding biological effects have remained unrevealed until present. During our search for suitable sugar-based nanoparticles, we found that one of the most popular traditional Chinese food, pork braised in brown sauce, contained remarkable carbon nanoparticles (carbon nanodots). The carbon nanodots were generated due to the particular production process, including the stir-frying and caramelization of sugar in hot vegetable oil, similar to dehydration-condensation reactions of organic molecules at high temperatures (150–250 ℃) in common carbon nanodots syntheses [8,9]. Therefore, we followed the procedure for preparation of pork braised in brown sauce, and extracted water-dispersible sugar-originated carbon nanodots (sCND) from the caramelized sucrose to examine their biological effects and biosafety (Scheme S1).

The extracted sCND were uniformed polymer-like nanoparticles (2–8 nm) with elemental compositions of carbon (43.57 wt%), oxygen (55.63 wt%), nitrogen (5.23 wt%) and hydrogen (6.37 wt%) ( Fig. 1a and b, Figs. S1 and S2). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of the particles revealed a graphitic carbon core with well-resolved lattice fringes of the typical (100) plane (d-spacing = 0.21 nm) [10,11] (Figs. 1c and S1). Different surface groups such as CH, CO, and OH were detected on these carbon nanoparticles, and these nanoparticles could be fragmentated into a series of ion peaks with M/Z between 212 and 1572, which therefore suggested the macromolecular structure (Figs. 1d, 1e and S2 and S3). Considering the sequential dehydration-condensation of sucrose to form caramel in the hot vegetable oil followed by the Ostwald ripening crystallization from the core to the surfaces [12,13], we claimed a core-shell structure of sCND based on polymer-coated graphitic carbon. The hydrophilic shells endowed sCND with a zeta potential of −28.6 mV and excellent water dispersity (Fig. S4). As a result, the sCND exhibited a DLS particle size (7.8 nm) which was consistent with the TEM observation, if hydration was considered. A transparent appearance even after a centrifugation at 16,000 rpm for 10 min suggested the good water-dispersity (Fig. S4).

Owing to the water-dispersible uniformed nanodots, we firstly investigated their optical properties. sCND exhibited a linearly concentration-dependent π-π * transition absorbance at 270 nm due to the excellent water-solubility (Fig. S5), similar to fluorescent carbon nanoparticles [11,14]. Accordingly, sCND emitted full-color fluorescence from blue to red through increasing the excitation wavelength (Figs. 1f and S6). The representative fluorescence of sCND was clearly observed under a 365 nm UV lamp and distinctly determined via a fluorescence imaging study with 430 nm excitation (Fig. 1g and S5). The fluorescence was stable and the intensity was maintained upon exposure to solutions with 10% FBS, neutral pH, different ionic strength, or different UV irradiation time periods (Figs. S7–S10). Consequently, sCND which were rapidly taken up by glioma cells in concentration- and time-dependent manner (Figs. 1h, S11 and S12), could serve as multicolor bioimaging probe via the apparent blue, green, and red emissions upon variable excitations at 405, 456, and 488 nm, respectively. This imaging facilitated the tracking of the intracellular behavior of sCND, where the increased co-localization of sCND with the acid organelles and the reduced overlapping with mitochondria along with time suggested the endosome-lysosome uptake pathway and the efficient mitochondrial escape mechanism, respectively (Fig. S13).

Based on the favorable imaging properties and the relatively small size of the highly water-dispersible nanoparticles, we expected the potential of sCND for advanced in vivo fluorescence imaging in tumors, especially glioma where the BBB exhibits higher permeability for small particles (<15 nm) [15]. To verify this hypothesis, sCND were administered intravenously into orthotopic glioma-bearing mice. It was observed that sCND could overcome the BBB and gradually accumulated in the brain tumor region. The brightest fluorescence imaging of the tumor was observed at 465 nm excitation 30 min post-injection (Figs. 1i–l). Then, the sCND began to be eliminated from the body, where the high accumulation of sCND within the liver, lung and specifically the kidney indicated the maximum elimination by these organs (Fig. 1j). Ex-vivo imaging confirmed the distinct glioma-targeting ability of sCND (Figs. 1k and l). Although the hydrophilic polymer surfaces and small sizes of sCND facilitated the enhanced passive targeting via the enhanced permeation retention (EPR) effect, we sought about additional structure advantages of sCND and found the similar functional groups of sucrose-derived sCND with the D-glucose (Fig. S14). Because glucose is the natural ligand of the highly expressed GLUT1 on tumor cells, sCND might also be affected by the GLUT1-mediated glioma targeting. In order to prove this, a series of comparative assays were conducted. First, pre-incubation with glucose (50 mM and 100 mM) or glucose-containing complete medium (Com-M), blocking the GLUT1-mediated pathway (CYB), and using GLUT1-low expressing normal cells (1800) all inhibited the uptake of sCND and exhibited the relative weak fluorescence (Figs. S15 and 2a). Notably, there was a positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of blocking agents. Second, immunostaining of the GLUT1 via TRITC-labeled GLUT1 antibody clearly displayed the initial co-localization of sCND with GLUT1 on tumor cells and then gradual redistribution into the nucleus (Fig. 2b). This verified the GLUT1-mediated uptake of sCND into glioma cells. Moreover, the energy-independent cellular uptake of sCND also suggested the GLUT1-mediated transportation without energy consumption [7] (Fig. S16). Additionally, injection of sCND to the cells pre-adhered to a sensor surface for real-time QCM-D monitoring revealed a more significant frequency (F) decrease in GLUT1-high expressing U87 tumor cells than in normal 1800 cells, indicating higher uptake of sCND by U87 cells [16]. Furthermore, the dissipation (D) kept increasing in U87 cells, revealing the strong ligand-receptor interaction [17]. In contrast, the dissipation first increased and then decreased in 1800 cells, suggesting an unstable adhesion of sCND on normal cells possibly due to weak noncovalent interaction [18] (Fig. 2c). These observations confirmed the GLUT1-mediated targeting uptake of sCND by glioma. And sCND could be used for in vivo targeting fluorescence imaging of glioma.

Since tumors undergo changes in cellular metabolism with an enhanced glucose avidity (Warburg effect) supported by the highly expressed GLUT1 [6,7], the competition of sCND with glucose was considered to be able to specifically interfere the uptake of glucose into tumors. In support of this deduction, we observed inhibition of glucose uptake into glioma cells after incubation with increasing concentration of sCND (Fig. 2d). Accordingly, the gradual abatement of the relatively rich lactic acid in tumor cells (HepG2), which results from the anaerobic glycolysis, was realized via incubation with increasing amounts of sCND (Fig. 2e). However, this effect was not very pronounced in normal cells (HL 7702) due to the lower influence of sCND on glucose uptake into these cells (Fig. 2e). Meanwhile, we also monitored similar inhibitory effects on glucose uptake in vivo via 2-deoxy-2-[18F]fluoro-d-glucose ([18F]FDG)-contrasted PET images (Fig. 2f), where [18F]FDG signal in glioma was significantly reduced in sCND-pretreated mice when compared with control mice at 15 min post-treatment. Most [18F]FDG was concentrated around the glioma of sCND-pretreated mice, manifesting a concentration-dependent behavior due to the sCND-prevented uptake of glucose into the tumor. Desirably, this special prevention was still maintained after treatment for 30 min. Furthermore, we simulated the interaction between the GLUT1 and the glucose/sCND using molecular docking [19]. As expected, GLUT1 has a large active cavity (25 Å) with plentiful π-conjugated or porbital-unoccupied alkyl groups-linked amino acid residues such as tryptophan (TRP), histidine (HIS), proline (PRO) and isoleucine (ILE), etc. Glucose molecules can enter the cavity with special hydrogen bond/van der Waals interactions and thus achieve the transmembrane transport via this GLUT1 mediation ( Fig. 3a and b). Much interestingly, besides the glucose residue in sCND-guided interaction with GLUT1, sCND with sp2-hybridized carbon core had an extra interaction with GLUT1 via the π-π and π-alkyl system (Fig. 3c and d), which therefore possessed a much stronger binding (low-CDocker energy) to GLUT1 as compared to the pure glucose molecules (Fig. 3e). Notably, similar to the GLUT1-glucose interaction, sCND did not obviously affect the secondary structures of GLUT1 (Fig. 3g), indicating that sCND did not affect the activity of the glucose receptor. Nevertheless, owing to the approximate small-size of sCND with that of the active cavity of GLUT1, sCND could be locked in GLUT1 and thus blocked the glucose transport via their interaction with lower docker energy. In contrast, the hexokinase (HK), which has an obvious interaction with glucose (-CDocker energy of ~28 kcal/mol) and catalyzes the phosphorylation of glucose for the subsequent glycolysis in cells, could not interact with sCND. A possible reason was the much smaller size of the active cavity (~5 Å) of HK than that of sCND. In this situation, sCND, despite the surface glucose residues, could not be phosphorylated for the subsequent energy metabolism (Fig. S17). Therefore, sCND could inhibit the cellular glucose uptake into cells by GLUT1 mediation, and consequently reduce the cellular energy supply and lactic acid production (Fig. 3f).

To examine the influence of sCND binding to GLUT1 on the cell membrane, the membrane properties including the roughness, rigidity and fluidity were measured using an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Figs. 3h, 18–19) and a fluorescence recovery after photobleaching technique (FRAP) (Figs. 3i and S20). Different from the normal cells, the tumor cells had a more obvious increase of their roughness after sCND treatment, as indexed from the wavier surface with a higher Rq (root-mean-square). This suggested the massive embedding of sCND in the tumor cell membranes mediated by the overexpressed GLUT1 on the tumor cell surfaces. Similarly, the membrane rigidity (indicated by Young’s modulus) after sCND treatment, was also more increased on tumor cells than that on the normal cells, confirming the influences of the massively embedded sCND on the cell membranes (Fig. S19). Correspondingly, the decreased membrane fluidity via the sCND treatment, reflected by the reduced diffusion coefficient (Diff Co) and prolonged half-time (t1/2) of fluorescence recovery (Fig. 3i), was clearly observed on tumor cells but not so obvious on normal cells, which further confirms the effects of sCND binding to overexpressed GLUT1. Interestingly, some of the studies [20] have been shown that nanomaterials interfered with the cell membranes could reduce the glucose uptake and the drug efflux of tumor cells during the therapy.

Because sCND acted as inhibitors of glucose uptake but could not contribute to neither the elevation of blood sugar concentration in vivo nor increase of body weight, rather different from glucose and sugar (Fig. S21), it was expected that the sCND pretreatment would impair the tumor cells and enhance chemotherapy via decreasing the energy supply to the tumor cells. To examine this effect, the cell indexes were first measured, where the addition of sCND to the pre-starved cells clearly reduced the cell growth. And this trend was more significant in glioma U87 cells than in normal 1800 cells because of the elevated glucose avidity of tumor cells ( Fig. 4a). Correspondingly, the rapid proliferation of HepG2 tumor cells under glucose incubation was remarkably inhibited by sCND (Figs. 4b and d). At a relatively larger dose and longer time incubation, the sCND could even cause the obvious cytotoxicity to HepG2 tumor cells but almost no effects on normal HL 7702 cells, suggesting the much more severe lack of glucose in GLUT1-overexpressed tumor cells via sCND-mediated inhibition. Furtherly, the cytotoxicity caused by glucose deficiency was much more evident through incubation with glucose-free (GLU-free) medium. In this situation, the apoptosis was also emerged much faster on HepG2 tumor cells than that on some normal HL 7702 cells, revealing the much more sensitivity of tumor cells to glucose deficiency. Nevertheless, being different from that incubation with complete medium, the apoptosis was induced independent of sCND on both HepG2 tumor cells and normal HL 7702 cells in GLU-free media (Fig. 4b and d). This could be attributed to the no glucose in extracellular environment for the sCND-mediated blocking. And it also proved the fake glucose of sCND without glycometabolism effects. To explore this specific impairing to tumor cells by sCND, the ATP production after different treatments was also measured. Normally, tumor cells need a higher ATP production to sustain their quicker proliferation than normal cells. Therefore, treatment by sCND caused an obvious decrease of ATP production in tumor cells with the increased concentrations (Fig. 4c). However, this effect was not obvious in normal cells due to their low-expression of GLUT1, which therefore was less interfered by sCND. Particularly, this sCND-inhibited ATP production was more pronounced under the pre-starvation, also revealing the sensitive influence of sCND to glucose energy supply in tumor cells through GLUT1-mediation (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the ATP production under the GLU-free medium was also strongly inhibited by sCND in tumor cells. This might be attributed to the fake glucose of sCND without glycometabolism effects, which however could act as the fakement under the glucose-destitute environment to interfere the glycolysis in the glucose-avid tumor cells [21]. However, this effect was less significant in normal cells since their glucose consumption is relatively lower than the tumor cells. These further revealed the targeting impairing of sCND to tumor cells.

In combination with the chemotherapeutic temozolomide (TMZ), the pretreatment with increased sCND augmented the cytotoxicity of TMZ chemotherapy (Figs. 4e and f). Pretreatment with an sCND concentration of 400 μg/mL remarkably reduceed the IC50 of TMZ from 50.12 μg/mL in case of the monotherapyto 34.67 μg/mL in case of the sCND-enhanced chemotherapy (30.83% off) (Fig. S22A). The increase of potency is beneficial for tumor therapy since the required lower dosing can help to prevent side effects. Next, glioma-bearing nude mice were treated with TMZ and sCND either alone or in combination. Correspondingly, there was no obvious weight loss via the combined treatments, especially under the increased sCND concentrations (Fig. 4g). And it was found that monotherapy of sCND did not suppress the tumor apparently and only combination with TMZ could produce more glioma apoptosis and prolong the survival time more remarkably (Fig. 4h and i) compared with the control groups and the TMZ groups. Therefore, sCND could impair the tumor glycometabolism (energy supply) through inhibiting the tumor glucose uptake and thus served as a potential agent to enhance the chemosensitivity. Since an important mechanism for the development of chemoresistance is based on drug efflux by ABC (ATP-binding cassette) transporters, such as P-glycoprotein, which are depended on the intracellular ATP [22,23], the impairing of energy supply via sCND could be a universal strategy for enhanced tumor therapy.

Since most kinds of cancer cells over-express GLUT1 and have a high need for glucose, we expected that the sCND-promoted chemosensitivity was effectual on various cancer cell types. Thus, treatments with sCND and an alternative anticancer drug doxorubicine (DOX) either alone or in combination were examined on several other cancer cells. Notably, the combined treatment mediated an elevated cytotoxicity on all the cancer cell types as compared with the single treatment, confirming the sCND-promoted chemotherapy. Especially, this promotion was much more significant on HepG2, MCF-7 and A549 cells. This might be attributed to the relatively higher GLUT1 expression to block the glucose uptake on these cells (Fig. S23), where even single sCND treatment also caused a slight cell growth inhibition and the difference sensitivity of tumor cells to DOX (Fig. 4j). While for the less sensitive cells, particularly the GLUT1-overexpressing PANC-1, we could further enhance the chemosensitivity via pretreating with sCND for 24 h, attributing to the persistent inhibition of cellular glucose uptake (Fig. 4j). This verified the sCND-promoted chemosensitivity. Subsequently, the breast cancer cells (MCF-7) and the normal breast cells (MCF-10) were subjected to different treatments either alone or in combination under different starvations (diverse energy supplies) (Fig. 4k). It was found that the combined treatment or even the single treatment produced the enhanced cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells under the starvations, and especially under the complete starvation conditions, indicating that the better chemosensitization of sCND needs the assistance of reasonable treatment strategy. In comparison, this enhancement was less significant on MCF-10 cells. These evidenced the controllability and cancer-targeted ability of sCND-promoted chemotherapy due to its special mechanism of inhibiting the tumor-avid energy supply via preventing the GLUT1-mediated glucose uptake.

Furthermore, liver cancer cells (HepG2) were treated with sorafenib alone or in combination with sCND. We also observed the sCND-promoted chemosensitivity, where the sCND (400 μg/mL) remarkably reduced the IC50 of sorafenib from 66.07 μg/mL in case of the monotherapy to 39.81 μg/mL in case of sCND-enhanced chemotherapy (40.70% off) (Fig. S22B). Consistently, the sCND-assisted therapeutic outcome of oral sorafenib tablets for in vivo liver tumor was tested to fully demonstrate the effect of chemotherapeutic sensitization of sCND. In the study of intravenous sCND to improve the efficacy of chemotherapy with sorafenib ( Fig. 5a), all sCND combination treatments were well tolerated without apparent body weight loss due to the biocompatibility of sCND and the relatively low dose of chemotherapeutic drug (Fig. 5b). And treatment with sorafenib and sCND in combination caused pronounced tumor apoptosis and growth inhibition (Fig. 5c–f). Moreover, this synergistic promotion of the therapeutic effects was significantly enhanced when sCND were pre-injected around 0–1 h ahead (the therapeutic strategy E and F in Fig. 5a), accounting for the optimal administration window to impair the tumor via sCND treatment. Besides, we also found that pre-starvation of tumor-bearing mice for 12 h (the therapeutic strategy G in Fig. 5a) contributed to an elevated therapeutic outcome, confirming the enhanced chemotherapy sensitivity via regulating the energy supply (Fig. 5c–f). Further,considering the preparation of sCND by a preparation inspired from traditional cooking, the chemotherapy augmentation was investigated with oral administration of sCND with sorafenib (Fig. 5g). Similar therapeutic enhancement with good tolerance was obtained by intravenous sCND-mediated combined treatments. Howbeit, the oral administration of sCND 2 h in advance was needed to optimize the chemotherapy due to the relatively slow accumulation into tumor area via oral administration (Fig. 5h–k). Therefore, oral administration of sCND under the optimal windows can also enhance the chemotherapy sensitivity.

For the clinical use of a novel nanoparticle, the detailed investigation of its biosafety is essential. Because of the peculiar synthesis which was inspired by the traditional food as well as no obvious effect on the secondary structure of GLUT1, sCND were expected to be edible at therapeutic doses with good biosafety. First, during a single incubation for 6 h, sCND had no obvious cytotoxicity to especially the normal cells even at the concentration of 3.2 mg/mL (Fig. S24). Second, the interaction between sCND and GLUT1 did not affect the expression of glucose transporter on cell membrane apparently (Fig. S25). Third, various biochemistry and hematology analysis, particularly for kidney and liver function markers, revealed that neither hepatic/nephric toxicities nor other blood dyscrasia were evident after intravenous injection of sCND even at a dose of 200 mg/kg within 12 weeks ( Figs. 6 and Fig. S26). Further tests of humoral immune parameters suggested no immunoreactions of sCND, similar to physiological saline (Fig. S26-S29). Moreover, both the H&E stained images and apoptosis results indicated no damage effects (no apparent histopathological abnormalities or lesion) of sCND to the major organs at various time points post-injection (Fig. S27-S30). These findings suggested the excellent biosafety of the as-synthesized sCND. Most importantly, sCND were extracted from a reaction mixture similar to the food eaten all the year round, and the only extraction solvent residue (chloroform) was authenticated to meet the Food and Drug Standards (Fig. S29). Therefore, sCND is expected to possess good biosafety, which might be of great importance for the clinical use.

In summary, sCND was prepared via the typical procedure of pork braised in brown sauce. In this procedure, the sucrose was first caramelized into water-dispersibility carbon nanodots in the hot vegetable oil and then crystallized from core to shell by Ostwald ripening. Because of the uniform small size, good water dispersity, stable and tunable fluorescence emissions, sCND could permeate the BBB for multicolor fluorescence imaging of glioma. Meanwhile, owing to the ingenious synthesis, sCND were characterized with some glucose residues on the surface, which therefore could specifically accumulate in glioma via the GLUT1-mediated targeting, and accordingly achieve the inhibition of glioma, and other cancer types, growth as well as enhance the chemotherapy via interfering the tumor-avid glucose uptake and then blocking the tumor energy metabolism. Moreover, originated from the classical food eaten all the year round, sCND factually possessed excellent long-term biosafety including low cytotoxicity, no hematotoxicity and no mediation of tissue damages. Consequently, this work provides an indirect method to produce the carbon nanodot with excellent biosafety, which will pave a robust way for the practical clinical applications.

Since tumors undergo changes in cellular metabolism and survive with much higher glucose uptake via their over-expressed GLUT1 (Warburg effects) [6,24–26], inhibiting the glucose uptake into tumor cells will reduce the energy supply and thus weaken their activity. In this case, traditional therapy including chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or other physical and chemical therapies will be more effective to kill the cancer cells [21] (Scheme 1). However, no valid nanoparticles with sufficient biosafety can directly block the tumor-glucose uptake for enhanced tumor therapy in the clinic until now. Herein, a cooking skill of pork braised in brown sauce was used to prepare sugar-originated carbon nanodots (sCND) (Scheme S1). According to this procedure, the sucrose was dehydrated and condensed to form a caramel in the hot vegetable oil, where the carbonization or crystallization began from the core to shell by Ostwald ripening. Thus, a hydrophilic core-shell nanoparticle with uniformed size and polymer surface with possible glucose residues was obtained. The sCND exhibited stable and adjustable fluorescence emissions possibly due to a series of excited states, as similar to those of some other specially prepared carbon nanodots [8,27]. Attributing to the small sizes and high water dispersity as well as their special surface residues, sCND could overcome the BBB and specifically accumulate in tumor cells via elevated EPR effect and GLUT1 mediation, achieving bioimaging. Owing to the ultra-small size of sCND with the glucose residues, sCND were found to have an obvious interaction with GLUT1 but could not be phosphorylated for subsequent glycolysis. As a result from the competitive transportation of sCND and glucose via the same GLUT1, sCND could block the glucose uptake and thus decrease the energy supply into cells. In this situation, the cells would be impaired in terms a decreased membrane fluidity and alterative intracellular microenvironment, which therefore was much more fragile and sensitive to other therapies including chemotherapy. Because cancer cells proliferate more quickly with much higher energy-demanded glucose uptake via their over-expressed GLUT1 (Warburg effects) as compared with normal cells, sCND-mediated impairing and chemotherapy enhancement were much more significant on tumor cells but without obvious affects to normal cells. More importantly, these particular functions of sCND were much more effective when the tumor cells were pre-starved, because it aggravated the deficiency of glucose in tumor cells. Therefore, the sCND-assisted chemotherapy was examined on various cells via different modes and times of administration, which produced synergistically enhanced therapeutic outcomes according to the sCND-mediated inhibition via optimizing the treatment windows. Because of the normal glycometabolism with relatively low-expressed GLUT1 and low energy demand on normal cells, sCND had low toxicity to normal cells even at a high concentration under physiological glucose environment. Moreover, sCND around therapeutic dose showed no side-effects to the blood and normal organs. Additionally, sCND were expected to be edible with excellent biocompatibility and factually inherited long-term biosafety since the peculiar synthesis and the entirely origination from the traditional food without over-limited toxic residues. Therefore, sCND will be a powerful theranostic agent for most likely clinical applications.

The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. Professor, Rongqin Huang conceived the original idea, led the project, tailored all details and supervised the whole work. Yi Wang, Taotao Huo, Huiling Jiang and Yibo Xie performed most of the assays. Xiaoyi Zhang, Huifang Nie, Yafeng Yang and Min Qian contributed to chemical synthesis. Wenshuai Li, Tingting Hao, Wei Guo, Yanhui Qin, Jinlong Shi and Wei Shi contributed to cell and animal evaluation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81861138040, 31922044, 81773280 and 81974453), Program of Shanghai Academic Research Leader (20XD1420500), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (19ZR1471600), Jiangsu Provincial Science and Technology Department Social Development–Clinical Frontier Technology (BE2020769),and Minhang Hospital-Fudan Pharmacy Joint Project (RO-MY201802). We thank our collaboration partners Dr. Ulrich Lächelt and Prof. Ernst Wagner (Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität; DFG-NSFC Joint Sino-German Research Project 410149116) for fruitful discussions and also editing of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nantod.2021.101200.
